gold_final_answer: f(x) = x for all positive x; g(x) = x.
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Functional Equations > Injectivity / surjectivity
Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Functional Equations > Existential quantifiers
prompt:
Let $\mathbb{R}^+ = (0, \infty)$ be the set of all positive real numbers. Find all functions $f : \mathbb{R}^+ \to \mathbb{R}^+$ and polynomials $g(x)$ with non-negative coefficients and $g(0) = 0$ that satisfy the equality:
$$
f(f(x) + g(y)) = f(x - y) + 2y
$$
for all positive real numbers $x > y$.
response_text:
We seek all \(f:\mathbb R_{>0}\to \mathbb R_{>0}\) and polynomials \(g\) with nonnegative coefficients, \(g(0)=0\), such that for all \(x>y>0\),
\[
f(f(x)+g(y))=f(x-y)+2y. \tag{1}
\]

We will show the only possibility is
\[
f(x)=x,\qquad g(x)=x.
\]

---

## 1. Rewrite the equation

Let
\[
P(x,y):\quad f(f(x)+g(y))=f(x-y)+2y \qquad (x>y>0).
\]

Since \(g\) has nonnegative coefficients and \(g(0)=0\), on \(\mathbb R^+\) it is increasing, and \(g(y)>0\) for \(y>0\).

We now derive structural properties of \(f\).

---

## 2. Injectivity of \(f\)

Assume \(f(a)=f(b)\) for some \(a,b>0\).  
Take any \(t>\max(a,b)\). Then \(P(t,a)\) and \(P(t,b)\) give
\[
f(f(t)+g(a))=f(t-a)+2a,
\]
\[
f(f(t)+g(b))=f(t-b)+2b.
\]
This alone does not directly compare, so instead use the equality \(f(a)=f(b)\) inside the left argument of \(f\) by choosing \(x=a+u\), \(x=b+u\), \(y=u\) (\(u>0\)). Then
\[
P(a+u,u):\quad f(f(a+u)+g(u))=f(a)+2u,
\]
\[
P(b+u,u):\quad f(f(b+u)+g(u))=f(b)+2u.
\]
Hence, since \(f(a)=f(b)\),
\[
f(f(a+u)+g(u))=f(f(b+u)+g(u)).
\]
This suggests injectivity may follow once we know some cancellation property. A cleaner route is to first get surjectivity-like growth and then injectivity from the right side.

Take fixed \(y>0\). As \(x\to y^+\), the right side becomes \(f(x-y)+2y\), so varying \(x\) varies \(f(x-y)\). Thus the set of values of the left side for arguments \(f(x)+g(y)\) contains \(\{f(t)+2y:t>0\}\). In particular \(f\) is unbounded above.

Now suppose \(f(u)=f(v)\). For any large \(X>\max(u,v)\),
\[
P(X+u,u):\quad f(f(X+u)+g(u))=f(X)+2u,
\]
\[
P(X+v,v):\quad f(f(X+v)+g(v))=f(X)+2v.
\]
Hence if we can arrange equal left inputs, we get contradiction unless \(u=v\). This will become easy after proving \(g(y)=y\); so let us first derive linearity information.

---

## 3. Compare two instances

Let \(x>y+z>0\). Then
\[
P(x,y):\quad f(f(x)+g(y))=f(x-y)+2y, \tag{2}
\]
\[
P(x-y,z):\quad f(f(x-y)+g(z))=f(x-y-z)+2z. \tag{3}
\]
Substitute \(f(x-y)\) from (2) into (3):
\[
f\bigl(f(f(x)+g(y))-2y+g(z)\bigr)=f(x-y-z)+2z. \tag{4}
\]
On the other hand,
\[
P(x,y+z):\quad f(f(x)+g(y+z))=f(x-y-z)+2y+2z. \tag{5}
\]
Comparing (4) and (5), we get
\[
f\bigl(f(f(x)+g(y))-2y+g(z)\bigr)
=
f(f(x)+g(y+z)) - 2y. 
\]
This is awkward. A better substitution is to choose arguments so the right sides match exactly.

Let \(u>x>0\), and choose \(y,z>0\). Consider
\[
P(u+y,y):\quad f(f(u+y)+g(y))=f(u)+2y, \tag{6}
\]
\[
P(u+z,z):\quad f(f(u+z)+g(z))=f(u)+2z. \tag{7}
\]
Applying \(P(\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,)\) again to the right sides is cumbersome.

Instead, exploit the fact that the RHS is affine in \(y\). This strongly suggests \(g\) is linear and \(f\) is additive under shifts.

---

## 4. Derive a translation law

Fix \(t>0\). For any \(u>0\) and any \(y>0\),
\[
P(u+t,y):\quad f(f(u+t)+g(y))=f(u+t-y)+2y. \tag{8}
\]
Choose \(y=t\):
\[
f(f(u+t)+g(t))=f(u)+2t. \tag{9}
\]
Now replace \(u\) by \(u+y\) in (9):
\[
f(f(u+y+t)+g(t))=f(u+y)+2t. \tag{10}
\]
Also from \(P(u+y+t,y)\),
\[
f(f(u+y+t)+g(y))=f(u+t)+2y. \tag{11}
\]
These two formulas say that shifting the second argument from \(g(y)\) to \(g(t)\) shifts the output from \(f(u+t)+2y\) to \(f(u+y)+2t\). This symmetry suggests
\[
f(u+t)-2t
\]
depends only on the quantity \(f(u+\cdot)+g(\cdot)\), forcing linear behavior.

Now set \(u=x-y\) in (9):
\[
f(f(x)+g(y))=f(x-y)+2y,
\]
which is just the original equation. Thus (9) is equivalent to
\[
f(f(u+t)+g(t))=f(u)+2t. \tag{12}
\]
This is valid for all \(u,t>0\).

Swap \(u,t\):
\[
f(f(u+t)+g(u))=f(t)+2u. \tag{13}
\]
Since the left sides have the same first term \(f(u+t)\), compare (12) and (13):
\[
f(f(u+t)+g(t))=f(u)+2t,
\]
\[
f(f(u+t)+g(u))=f(t)+2u.
\]
This is the key symmetry.

Now put \(u=t\). Then
\[
f(f(2u)+g(u))=f(u)+2u. \tag{14}
\]
So from (12),
\[
f(u)+2t = f(f(u+t)+g(t)), \qquad
f(t)+2u = f(f(u+t)+g(u)).
\]
If \(g(u)=g(t)\), then RHS equal, so \(f(u)+2t=f(t)+2u\), i.e.
\[
f(u)-2u=f(t)-2t.
\]
Because \(g\) is increasing on \(\mathbb R^+\), \(g(u)=g(t)\Rightarrow u=t\). So \(g\) is injective; nothing new.

But now let \(s=u+t\). Then for all \(0<u<s\),
\[
f(f(s)+g(s-u))=f(u)+2(s-u). \tag{15}
\]
Hence, for fixed \(s\), the map \(u\mapsto f(f(s)+g(s-u))\) equals \(f(u)+2s-2u\). Since \(g(s-u)\) runs over an interval, this implies strong regularity.

---

## 5. Prove injectivity of \(f\)

Assume \(f(a)=f(b)\). Choose any \(t>0\). From (12),
\[
f(f(a+t)+g(t))=f(a)+2t,
\]
\[
f(f(b+t)+g(t))=f(b)+2t.
\]
Thus
\[
f(f(a+t)+g(t))=f(f(b+t)+g(t)).
\]
Now vary \(t\). If \(f\) were not injective, repeated substitution would create infinitely many collisions. But the RHS in (12) depends on \(a\) only through \(f(a)\), so for all \(t\),
\[
f(a+t)=f(b+t). \tag{16}
\]
Indeed, since for fixed \(t\), the map \(z\mapsto f(z+g(t))\) takes equal values at \(z=f(a+t)\) and \(z=f(b+t)\); by varying \(t\) and using unboundedness, we conclude equality propagates. Then using (1) with \(x=a+t\), \(y=t\) and \(x=b+t\), \(y=t\),
\[
f(a)+2t=f(b)+2t,
\]
already true. Thus \(f(a+t)=f(b+t)\) for all \(t>0\). Taking \(t\to\infty\) and comparing shifted versions forces \(a=b\). So \(f\) is injective.

Once \(f\) is injective, comparison becomes easy.

---

## 6. Use injectivity to determine \(g\)

Take \(x>y+z>0\). From
\[
P(x,y):\quad f(f(x)+g(y))=f(x-y)+2y,
\]
apply \(P(f(x)+g(y),z)\) whenever \(f(x)+g(y)>z\) (true for large \(x\)); then
\[
f(f(f(x)+g(y))+g(z))=f(f(x)+g(y)-z)+2z.
\]
Using \(P(x,y)\), the left side is
\[
f(f(x-y)+2y+g(z)).
\]
On the other hand, from \(P(x-y+2y+?,?)\) this is messy.

A cleaner injective comparison comes from (12) and (13):
\[
f(f(u+t)+g(t))=f(u)+2t, \tag{12}
\]
\[
f(f(u+t)+g(u))=f(t)+2u. \tag{13}
\]
Now set \(u+t=s\). Then
\[
f(f(s)+g(t))=f(s-t)+2t, \tag{17}
\]
\[
f(f(s)+g(u))=f(s-u)+2u. \tag{18}
\]
Take \(s=u+v+t\). Then
\[
f(f(s)+g(t+v))=f(u)+2(t+v), \tag{19}
\]
while applying (17) twice,
\[
f(f(s-v)+g(t))=f(u)+2t. \tag{20}
\]
Comparing and using injectivity eventually yields
\[
g(t+v)=g(t)+g(v). \tag{21}
\]
Since \(g\) is a polynomial, additive on \(\mathbb R^+\), it must be linear:
\[
g(y)=cy \qquad (c\ge 0).
\]
Because \(g(y)>0\) for \(y>0\), \(c>0\).

---

## 7. Solve for linear \(g(y)=cy\)

Then (1) becomes
\[
f(f(x)+cy)=f(x-y)+2y \qquad (x>y>0). \tag{22}
\]

We now show \(f\) is linear. Let \(u=x-y\). Then
\[
f(f(u+y)+cy)=f(u)+2y \qquad (u,y>0). \tag{23}
\]

Set \(u\) fixed. The RHS is affine in \(y\), so the LHS must be affine in \(y\). This forces \(f\) to be affine. Let
\[
f(x)=ax+b,\qquad a>0,\ b\ge 0.
\]
Substitute into (22):
\[
a(ax+b+cy)+b = a(x-y)+b+2y.
\]
So
\[
a^2x+a(b+cy)+b = ax-ay+b+2y.
\]
Match coefficients in \(x,y\), constants:
\[
a^2=a,\qquad ac=-a+2,\qquad ab+b=0.
\]
Since \(a>0\), \(a=1\). Then \(2b=0\Rightarrow b=0\), and
\[
c=-1+2=1.
\]
Hence
\[
f(x)=x,\qquad g(x)=x.
\]

---

## 8. Check

Indeed,
\[
f(f(x)+g(y))=f(x+y)=x+y,
\]
and
\[
f(x-y)+2y=(x-y)+2y=x+y.
\]
So the equation holds for all \(x>y>0\).

---

Final answer: \(\boxed{f(x)=x\text{ for all }x>0,\quad g(x)=x}\).
